text: Alumni, your terpmail may be deleted if you don't take action Visit
author: cherry_chocolate_

text:
A last call email went out today stating your terpmail account, including Gmail, would be deleted in 10 days if you are using more than 15gb of storage. While some IT help pages say they will delete Google Drive first, it may have changed because the email says they will delete your whole account.
I hope this post reaches someone who doesn't check their inbox all the time. It would be horrible to lose your files and even worse to lose your email address.
author: cherry_chocolate_

text: Ya'll remember when they promised lifetime unlimited google drive with terpmail? What happened to that?
author: unknown

text: Google is the one who ended it. It seems like Google used it as a sales tactic against universities. After many schools were using it, they pulled out the rug.
author: unknown

text:
I honestly think it wasn't so much of a rug pull as a "oh wow we didn't think people would use so much storage."

Like to me "rug pull" implies that was always the plan. And maybe it was! I'm not 100 percent ruling that out.

But I think they made the offer while grossly underestimating how much storage universities would use.

Like when I was in school years and years ago (not UMD), after graduation I only had my email account for like 30 days before being deactivated. Storage isn't free.
author: unknown

text:
So if you're some exec at Google who figures, after graduation, universities tend to delete accounts, or you assume most alumni go off and get their own email. Then yeah it seems like it would be pretty easy to offer unlimited storage.

But when unlimited storage *changes the way universities handle accounts entirely* - and they all let students keep them forever, because it's unlimited so why not? I could see people not expecting that to happen.
author: unknown

text: Granted I could have told you that's *exactly* what would happen. Because that's always what happens when you offer unlimited anything. I remember when AOL stopped charging per hour and did a flat monthly rate and all the phone lines got jammed. Exact same thing, again and again.
author: unknown

text:
who figures, after graduation, universities tend to delete accounts, or you assume most alumni go off and get their own email

 Well the whole sales pitch is that this was the norm, but that by signing up with Google you would be able to let alumni keep their emails for free forever. So they definitely expected people to use the email.
author: unknown

text: Realistically, Google should have handled this better. They sold on a promise they couldn't keep in 2014, so they should have made the transition easier. They made the same mistake with the Pixel phones in 2016 yet they still honor it.
author: unknown

text: Cold storage tape is very cheap and they already have an operation for that. They could have made an "archive" functionality for accounts created before the policy change. Or at the very least not restrict school admins from accessing the storage recovery tools.
author: unknown

text: Yeah, I think keeping an email account forever, and keeping unlimited Google Drive forever, are really pretty different things. And the unfortunate situation is, these are all tied up into the same, overall "thing", using the same single bucket of storage. It takes forever to rack up a large email file so offering email for life is *doable* (though there's still some big-picture questions around why universities (not just UMD) offer this anymore).
author: unknown

text: If Google Workspace could implement per-service quotas? This would all be so much better. Set a Drive quota, set a Photos quota, done. Maybe we'd have to set a Gmail quota just to be safe, or set the overall account quota in addition to the per-service quotas. That would make our email for life policy a lot more doable, I think.
author: unknown

text: though there's still some big-picture questions around why universities (not just UMD) offer this anymore
author: unknown

text: I use my umd email for work related stuff. And even if I didn't I still would want it to exist for forever. Maybe I decide to apply to grad school 5 years after undergrad and want to look at my past connections, contact a professor for a reference using an email that is obviously from UMD, etc. If Google can give me gmail for free, then a university should be able to provide me with an email address for life out of the tuition I paid.
author: unknown

text:
Yeah. The other philosophy that some universities have is they provide resources to further your education. Things like computer labs, specialized equipment, libraries, student health center, recreational facilities - all part of a holistic approach to education.

And when you graduate, you lose access to most of that stuff. Some schools treat your Google/Microsoft/(whoever) account like one of those resources.
author: unknown

text:
I can see arguments for both. After graduation it basically becomes a personal account and for most other resources that wouldn't be appropriate. But historically email has been very cheap.

A few minor points that I feel are important:
author: unknown

text: Nothing is truly free. Gmail may not charge you but, they're performing a combination of data collecting, getting your eyeballs in front of ads, and hoping you like the service enough you'll pay for the higher tiers. There's always a cost somewhere and it's just an issue of who's paying.
author: unknown

text: Providing an email address for life can be incredibly cheap but how you implement that is pretty important. Running an email forwarding service would technically qualify as providing you an email address, right? That has zero storage costs. But... That's not what most people mean when they say "email" - they really mean "Google account."
author: unknown

text:
And the most important thing: we're not really discussing email, we're discussing Google workspace accounts. If this was just email I don't think we'd be in this situation at all, most of the terpmail storage is alumni Google Drive and Google Photos by a huge margin. It's roughly 75% Drive, 20% Photos, 5% Gmail.

The alumni are largely using Terpmail for data storage and not for communication.
author: unknown

text: On Google’s side, the benefit they give by offering the email for life is significant. I know I did most of my college work on Google docs, not word, so now they have a bigger foothold on the corporate office world. If the school used Microsoft 365 I might still be a word user.
author: unknown

text: And I do think Google bears some responsibility to make this process smoother even if you don’t consider that enough benefit for them. Promising alumni email for life and then taking it away could be very damaging if someone loses access to important emails.
author: unknown

text: I am altering the deal. Pray I don't alter it any further.
author: unknown

text:
Well there's some missing history here.

The policy is really "email for life" and probably dates back to the 90s.

So you had "email for life", then the University transitioned to Google, and at the time - Google promised universities unlimited storage. And since email was powered by Google and Google offered drive with unlimited storage - we no longer view it as "email for life," we view it as "Google for life." There's an important distinction there.
author: unknown

text:
Then every university racked up huge amounts of storage and Google decided to implement caps on university accounts. So here we are.

If you go through the history of any KB article about Terpmail it never actually mentions unlimited storage. It's specifically that you have the account.

So you had "Terpmail for life", which was powered by Google, and at the time Google offered unlimited storage, so a lot of people (rightly) sort-of combined these into "unlimited storage for life."
author: unknown

text: Just wanna get the overall backstory out there. It's basically a case of, email for life meets service offering unlimited storage and it all kind of merging into a single thing.
author: unknown

text: That’s not the history here. There wasn’t central IT until the 00’s before that there were different little groups that did different things. Terpmail, and with it email for life wasn’t until the 10’s (maybe even the 2nd half of the decade)
author: unknown

text: So yes, there wasn't really a centralized email system for students until pretty late. But UMD had some kind of "email for life" implementation as far back as 2005 (I overshot when saying it probably goes back to the 90s): it was accomplished by using umd.edu as a forwarding service - https://web.archive.org/web/20060901095824/http://www.helpdesk.umd.edu/topics/email/alumni/4697/
author: unknown

text:
It isn't the same system but the overall idea of having an email address for life predates Terpmail, which is the important part.

Email for life was already a thing, then Terpmail came along using Google. Since the email was powered by Google, Google had unlimited storage, it got corrupted to "unlimited storage for life" when it's really always been "email for life"
author: unknown

I'm not the official umdit account but I'm one of the people that will be doing the actual deletions.

We will delete the account and make a new one so ultimately you still get to hold on to the email address, though you'll likely need to reach out to IT to do a password reset.

text: The reason for this is Google counts Photos in your storage usage but Photos isn't actually a workspace application. With Gmail and Drive we can do all kinds of things, but we have literally no way to manage anybody's Google Photos account. I'm pretty disappointed that Google decided to count it as storage without giving us ways to manage it.
author: unknown

text: The original plan was to try to do things more piecemeal but when Google came back with no way to handle Photos - that really threw a wrench in things. And yeah, that's the only way to handle photos, is to delete the whole account.
author: unknown

text:
Also if your main storage issue is Google photos, use partner sharing to copy photos to a Gmail account. https://kb.uconn.edu/space/IKB/26399244303/Use+Partner+Sharing+to+Migrate+Your+Google+Photos

IT cannot do this or set this up for you, you have to do it. Again, Photos isn't a Workspace app.
author: unknown

text: Are you still doing piecemeal deletions of accounts that aren't over in photos storage? I know it may not be your call, but if possible you should consider trying the google drive deletions first to see if that gets an account under the limit, and then trying one more time to contact the accounts that are over on photos.
author: unknown

text:
It's not my call combined with a few other factors.

Say it was my call - there's not really a way to just say "wipe this one person's drive." You have to use a tool that deletes all their files, but it requires iterating over every single file and deleting it. For some accounts the number of files is so high it just won't ever finish.
author: unknown

text: Plus Photos storage accounts for a really good amount of the total storage used. Drive is still #1 but even if we *could* just easily delete all the drive data we'd still be over the amount of storage Google has allocated for us.
author: unknown

text:
Dang Google is doing people dirty with that. There's an easy way to delete a single service in the UI.

 it requires iterating over every single file and deleting it

 I bet they want you to rack up API calls trying to do this so you have to pay to exit. I'm sure we're not the first university to need this.

Terrapins Connect

Terrapins Connect. Terrapins Connect is the University's virtual platform with the goal of connecting Terps from across the world with one another for mentoring, networking and professional opportunities.

Whether you are looking to give or receive advice, Terrapins Connect provides a way to get involved. By joining the platform, you gain access to connect with thousands of fellow Terps, lively affinity and professional groups, formal and informal mentoring programs, access to career development resources and programs, and so much more. What are you waiting for? Get started today!

Get Started. Setting up your account is quick and easy! Once you do so, you’ll be able to expand your Terp network, grow professionally, explore new career opportunities, and so much more.

1. Go to the Terrapins Connect homepage.
2. Click the "Join Your Community" button.
3. Select a signup method (ex. LinkedIn, Email, SSO)
4. Choose your User Type (ex. "Student, Alumni, Faculty/Staff)
5. Fill out your background info (if you use LinkedIn, your information will auto-populate!)

You may need to wait for an admin to approve your account. If that's the case, you’ll receive an email once your account has been approved.

Once you have an account, watch our YouTube playlist, TC Tips with Ethan, to get a better understanding of the platform's features.

text: My Profile is Created...Now What?. Waiting to hear from a student or fellow alumni and not sure what else you can do on Terrapins Connect? Not to worry- there are many things you can do on the platform to stay connected and engaged. Still lost on how to create a profile? Watch Ethan's TC Tips on profiles.

Explore the Community. Under the Community tab, scroll through profiles or use the search and filter bar on the top of the screen to narrow your search. You can filter by location, major, industry, and more. When you've found someone to connect with, click the "Message" button to send a message or request a meeting. Learn more about networking with this video.

Build Off Your Skills. Terrapins Connect gives Terps access to exclusive development modules that will give even the most seasoned professionals more resources for their toolboxes. Explore these tabs to find tips on interviews, sample letters for various occasions, and personal development resources. Learn more with the Pathways TC Tip with Ethan.

Join a Group. Groups allow you to connect with others who share your interests. They're typically communities focused on particular colleges or interest areas, such as "Clark School of Engineering" or "Terps in Education" You can join a group to access select resources. Learn more about Groups in this video.

**Need assistance starting your own group?** Sure! Email Phil Lupov at plupov@umd.edu to learn more about starting a group.

Check Out Career Paths. Want to know what steps you need to take for a certain career? How much money you might make? How others got there before you? Explore Terrapin Connect’s new Career Paths feature which guides you through various career paths and connects you to Terps who have been there. Ethan walks you through the new feature in his latest TC Tips with Ethan video.

Explore the Alumni Job Board. The Terrapins Connect job board pulls jobs for alumni from the University Career Center, fellow alumni, and WayUp. Click through jobs to find Terps who have worked there and apply through Terrapins Connect. Learn how to best navigate these features with our Job Board video.

Manage Your Settings. Control how your account is set up, who can see your profile, when you’re available, and more. To access your settings, click the dropdown menu on your profile picture in the right hand corner of the screen. From there, click “My Preferences”. Then on the left hand side, toggle through each of the following options:

- **Notifications:** Control when and how you’re getting information from Terrapins Connect. Do you want an email when someone messages you on the platform? Or a text? Or both?
- **Privacy:** Manage how your profile can be seen by others.
- **Availability:** Decide your meeting availability with others in the community. You may choose if you are available and how many people contact you in one month.
- **Account:** Change your email address, password, or if needed, delete your account here.

- **Manage your Calendar:** Here, you have the option to privately sync your calendar to show availability.
- **Matching Quiz:** Update your matching quiz to get recommended users for connections.
Preferences can be confusing. Check out Ethan's video to walk

Frequently Asked Questions. How do I sign up?

Signing up is easy. Go to www.terrapinsconnect.umd.edu and click on "Join Our Community." Mentors and mentees can choose to sign in through LinkedIn, Facebook or email.

How long do I have to wait for account approval?

Our team does our best to approve users as quickly as possible. Please allow 24 business hours. If you are concerned, you can email alumni@umd.edu.

How do I change my email?

How do I change my email?

- Click on your profile image in the upper right and then select "My Preferences" from the drop-down.
- Select "Account" from the menu on the left.
- Update your email address and password under "Email ID and Password." Remember to save your changes!
- You will need to confirm your new email by clicking the link we will send to your new address; once you have done so, we will start sending notifications to your new email.

How do I reset my password?

How do I reset my password?

Learn how to reset your password whether or not you're logged in.

- I am logged into the platform and want to reset my password for security reasons
- I am logged out and can't get back in because I don't know my password!

I have a duplicate account, what do I do?

Did you receive a message that your info was connected to an existing account? Sounds like you might already have an account! Select the statement below that sounds most relevant.

- I am trying to create a new account.
- I am trying to update my already-created account.

How do I get connected with a mentor?

The program will recommend connections based on your profile information. You can also choose to browse the entire TerrapinsConnect community by industry, major, location and more. Once you decide to reach out to a mentor, send a request to become officially paired.

Once they accept, they can send you a meeting invite for a phone call or video chat. You will receive emails to let you know when you've received a message.

What if I am alum looking for mentorship?

Alumni can absolutely join seeking mentorship. As an alum, you can connect with other alumni in your city or industry of interest. If you are interested in mentoring students and gaining career advice, you can do both!

I am on Terrapins Connect but no one has contacted me. Why?

I am on Terrapins Connect but no one has contacted me. Why?

Make sure you have taken the matching quiz to set your preferences, fully and accurately filled out your profile and set your availability. This will make it easier for mentees to find you! Also, please remember it is a large platform and sometimes it may take a while for mentees to find you.

Additionally, check your spam folder in case of any lost messages. If you find messages are consistently appearing in spam, click the Help button in the bottom left of the Terrapins Connect platform.

Can I have an anonymous profile?

Can I have an anonymous profile?

Terrapins Connect does NOT have an anonymous profile option. We felt it was important for people to "see" the face of their mentor, especially since the initial contact between mentor and mentee is via email. If you need to remain anonymous but want to participate in this program, please contact meganjm@umd.edu.

Is my personal information secure?

Is my personal information secure?

Terrapins Connect is powered by PeopleGrove, which uses your information solely "for the purpose of providing the Service, identifying and communicating with you, responding to your requests/inquiries, and improving the Service." To learn more, view PeopleGrove's Privacy Policy.

What's the best way to use this resource?

What's the best way to use this resource?

Each individual will use this resource differently. Some will have specific professional development questions, some will want to speak with people employed in specific industries or at specific organizations, some may need to talk through making their resume more concise. It will depend on you and how you feel you can best utilize Terrapins Connect.

I do not want to be a mentor/mentee anymore. How do I delete my profile?

I do not want to be a mentor/mentee anymore. How do I delete my profile?

Please email alumni@umd.edu and explain you want your profile deleted from the system. We will remove you from the system within five business days.

Am I able to restrict the types of students I advise?

Am I able to restrict the types of students I advise?

We ask that you be open to all students on campus, as you never know how your experiences might related to them, even though they may not be an "expected" advisee (i.e.. an engineering student seeking advice from a lawyer or someone in the public sector).

Do I have to advise every student who contacts me?

Do I have to advise every student who contacts me?

No. You can set your availability to allow connection requests from the number of mentees that you are comfortable of. You can also deny any connection request if your schedule is full or you feel it is the wrong fit.

As a mentor, can I change my availability?

As a mentor, can I change my availability?

Yes. You can update your availability at any time by clicking the dropdown menu next to your icon in the top right. Select "My Preferences" and navigate to the "Availability" section in the left sidebar.

Can I use my @umd email after graduation?

For @umd email-related answers, please explore FAQs from the IT Service Desk.

Executive Coach. **_Fine is a Trap - You Deserve Better_**

**Fine is a Trap** was founded on the principle that no one feels “fine”. As humans, we have much stronger feelings than that, and if we’re saying we’re “fine”, we’re usually avoiding sharing what’s actually difficult. When we get willing to address the “fine”, we have the opportunity to create amazing!

Lauren is an **Executive Coach**, partnering one-on-one with corporate leaders (individually or company-sponsored) ready to overcome burnout, create healthy boundaries, reduce busyness, develop executive skills, elevate or change their careers, and find joy in their work.

In addition, Lauren is a **Fractional Chief HR Officer** for companies with up to 100 employees supporting times of change. She offers strategic advisory services, employee training, organizational development planning, and systems selection and implementation.

She’s also a certified **Laughter Yoga Instructor**, offering virtual employee wellness sessions!

A former corporate Human Resources and Operations executive, Lauren has 25+ years' HR experience in for- and non-profit companies and has been coaching for more than 15 years. She’s managed, consulted with, and coached c-suite and emerging leaders in a variety of departments

. Lauren graduated from UMD in 1998 with a BA in Psychology, is a Professional Certified Coach (ICF PCC), a Certified Ontological Executive Coach, and holds Senior Professional in Human Resources (SPHR) and Senior SHRM Professional (SHRM SCP) certifications.

Using strategic thinking, creative solutions, and empathetic humor, she creates trusting and productive relationships.

Michael Seelman '94. _**Your Partner for Leadership Growth**_

Michael is the CEO of the Leadership Coach Group, a global leadership development firm providing executive coaching, team coaching, and training for leaders. He works with senior leaders to bring out their best performance, serve their people, and live a more fulfilled life. He has coached over 150 leaders on five continents – leveling up performance and achieving their goals.

Over two decades, Michael served in leadership roles at the White House, FBI, U.S. Department of Justice, and the U.S. Department of Homeland Security. He was often called upon to start up high profile offices and initiatives. At the White House he led a national gun violence reduction effort in collaboration with mayors and police chiefs.

At the FBI, he was the first global chief of employee communications, coaching FBI Director Bob Mueller and his leadership team on engaging with the 40,000+ workforce and organizational changes post-9/11 terrorist attacks. As Deputy Chief Knowledge Officer, he supervised the FBI’s knowledge management, innovation, data sharing, programs as well as outreach programs to Fortune 500 companies’ security executives. He also led teams of FBI, ATF, DEA, and U.S
. He also led teams of FBI, ATF, DEA, and U.S. Marshall officers reducing violence in the country’s most violent cities in partnership with local officials.

Corporate Networks. **The Spirit of Maryland at Work** 
We believe there’s something special about the University of Maryland that stays with you long after graduation. When Terps cross paths in the professional world, there’s an instant sense of connection—a shared experience that sparks collaboration, camaraderie and trust.

In today’s fast-paced work environment, it’s easy to focus on the to-do list and overlook the value of community, but strong connections are at the heart of a thriving workplace. Our alumni corporate networks build bonds through mentorship, fellowship and meaningful professional relationships. 
 
Explore the strength of the Maryland alumni network and discover what’s possible when Terps unite.

Surabi Kondapaka ‘18, Northrop Grumman. "It has been a privilege and joy to serve as the Vice President of the NGTerps Alumni Chapter.
